ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers potato croquette as a closest parent; the Italian-specific variant with Parmesan and mint is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers potato croquettes as a category; the specific Italian crocchette di patate with egg, mint, and Parmesan is not individually enumerated.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Italian potato croquettes; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crocchette di patate consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces, weighing around 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Potato croquettes, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 11374 base + preparation adjustment)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried potato croquettes (mashed potato base with egg and cheese, breaded and deep-fried). The Italian-specific formulation with Parmesan and mint is not individually listed in any primary regional database; values are estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue for potato croquettes (fried) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods entries for mashed potato, egg, and Parmesan cheese components. Energy and macros reflect a typical 100 g portion of the fried product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — potato products, fried/processed
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Italian crocchette di patate. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato croquettes / processed potato products (GI range 50–54 for breaded and fried potato preparations). The mashed-potato base, egg binding, and breadcrumb coating lower the GI relative to plain boiled potato (GI ~78) due to fat content slowing gastric emptying and the retrogradation of starch during cooling and frying. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 37.5) / 100 ≈ 19.5 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of crocchette di patate to one or two pieces per occasion, as the sodium from Parmesan cheese and seasoning contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and moderate carbohydrate density (~25 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume crocchette di patate in small portions (2–3 pieces), as the fat and protein content moderates the glycemic response, but the total carbohydrate load from a full restaurant serving (~37 g) warrants careful meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from Parmesan cheese and deep-frying oil, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat crocchette di patate as an occasional indulgence, limiting intake to 2–3 pieces, given the combined saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g) and sodium load that may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crocchette di patate are classic Italian potato croquettes made from a smooth mashed-potato base enriched with egg, grated Parmesan cheese, and fresh mint, shaped into oval or cylindrical portions, coated in breadcrumbs, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior delivers a satisfying crunch while the interior remains creamy and aromatic. The dish is a staple antipasto or side dish in Italian cuisine, particularly in southern Italy and Sicily, where street-food versions are widely enjoyed. The Parmesan adds umami depth and calcium, while the mint provides a distinctive herbal freshness uncommon in non-Italian croquette traditions. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 218 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 25 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), moderated by the fat and protein content slowing gastric emptying. The sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) is clinically relevant for hypertensive individuals. The dish contains egg, milk (Parmesan), and gluten (breadcrumbs) and is therefore unsuitable for those with allergies to these ingredients. In a Singapore restaurant context, crocchette are typically served as a starter or sharing plate.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crocchette di patate?
What is the GI of potato croquettes?
What cheese is used in Italian crocchette?
What is the difference between crocchette and croquettes?

confidence_reason: No exact match for crocchette di patate with Parmesan and mint was found in any primary regional food composition database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central named entry). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue for fried potato croquettes and cross-referenced with component-level data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for processed/fried potato products. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy analogue source for fried potato croquettes. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary analogue source. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary analogue source. All other -1 fields reflect genuine absence of data in the cited source rather than zero values. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and nutritionally plausible for a breaded, deep-fried potato product. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for this specific Italian formulation does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. The food is served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore (Boat Quay) in Singapore at SGD 16.24 per portion. Multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese) are null because this is an Italian dish with no established local-language names in those languages; the romanized Italian name 'Crocchette' is captured in romanized_local and aliases.
